data.title: The RealReal: Strong Customer Adds, But The Stock Has Run Too Hot (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
With the S&P 500 continuing to dance around all-time highs, I continue to advocate for two main portfolio moves: rotating out of momentum-driven winners that delivered big gains in 2024, and allocating more of our portfolios toward "growth at a reasonable price" stocks. Often, this means jumping into more speculative rebound plays: but the trick is when to lock in gains when these moves turn successful.

The RealReal (NASDAQ:REAL), an online consignment marketplace, has been a very positive trade over the past year. The struggling e-commerce name has now seen its share prices nearly quadruple over the past year as it successfully exited its direct-sales business and returned its focus on pure consignment. And yet, the stock fell ~15% after reporting Q4 results - an indication that the stock is running too hot against heightened expectations.

I last wrote a bullish note on The RealReal in November, when the stock was trading in the mid-$3s. Since then, my stake in The RealReal has nearly doubled, and I'm ready to pull the plug and retreat to the sidelines as the stock comes down, and wait for a better price to re-enter. The company's Q4 results showed continuing encouraging signs on active buyer uptake and GMV expansion, but most of this strength is already priced into The RealReal's stock. As such, I'm downgrading my stance on The RealReal to neutral.

At current share prices, I now see a more balanced bull and bear case for The RealReal. On the positive side for the company:

  • Smart decisions to focus on consignments has improved The RealReal's margin profile. The company's conscious decision to eliminate direct-sales and unwind its inventory is proving successful. GMV is growing y/y again, and consignment sales are growing at an even quicker pace. In addition, the revenue mix shift toward consignment and away from direct sales has pushed The RealReal's gross margin profile above 70%.
  • Trusted name in luxury. The main specter of competition for every company in the real estate space is Amazon (AMZN) - and yet, The RealReal's focus on the luxury category positions the company in a category that Amazon has struggled to gain legitimacy in.

At the same time, however, I view a number of risks for The RealReal that balance out its opportunities:

  • Tough macro conditions for luxury. Pinched by inflation and an uncertain economy, many luxury brands have reported weaker sales. This slowdown is being felt particularly in China, which historically has been the most enthusiastic buyer of luxury goods.
  • Competition. The RealReal has a wide swath of competition in its space, ranging from purchasing directly from fashion houses to other auction/consignment names like Postmark.

All in all, I see an opportunity to take gains on this trade and retreat to the sidelines until The RealReal's share price recedes further.

Q4 download

Let's now go through The RealReal's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

The RealReal's revenue grew 14% y/y to $164.0 million, missing Wall Street's expectations of $164.2 million (+15% y/y) by a hair. We do note, however, that The RealReal has continued to accelerate its revenue growth profile, sequentially improving 3 points from an 11% y/y growth rate in Q3.

Underpinning the company's improvement in revenue is continued recovery in GMV trends, which increased 12% y/y to $503 million. We note that GMV accelerated substantially versus 6% growth in Q3, which is a very healthy signal during the holiday quarter, especially when many luxury brands reported weaker-than-expected holiday results (suggesting that The RealReal and its customer base may have the potential to buck macro trends).

Also importantly as well, the company managed to improve its count of active buyers, adding 19k net-new buyers in the quarter to end with 408k active buyers who made a purchase in the trailing three months.

The company's primary strategy for growth consists of leaning more on its sales reps, which forge direct relationships with large-volume consignors. It views a healthy supply on its marketplace as the key constraint on revenue growth, and in FY24 the company experimented with new incentive structures to encourage better performance. The company notes that it's entering into FY25 with better sales rep retention, which could be a factor for better supply growth and overall expansion this year. Per CEO Rati Levesque's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Let's start with the growth playbook. The first component of our growth playbook is our sales team, which is a strategic differentiator. As a reminder, our sales team is made up of our luxury managers who work directly with our consignors to build trust and maximize supply.

Over the course of 2024, we spent time optimizing our incentive structure and elevating our sales team's experience. We better aligned the team's compensation with our overall company goal of driving profitable supply, focusing the team on value rather than units.

Better compensation alignment and elevating the employee experience have resulted in more supply value per luxury manager, lower attrition and higher sales team retention compared to last year. Today, more than half of our sales team has now been with The RealReal for over two years.

We expect these changes to drive results through deeper relationships with sellers, higher approval rating and a better seller experience. Notably, the value generated per sales rep in 2024 was up roughly 15% compared to the prior year."

The company notes that end customer sell-through also remains strong, with the majority of items selling within 90 days on the platform.

On the profitability front, the company improved gross margins by 40bps y/y to 74.4%, a reflection of higher marketplace take rates on growing GMV. Meanwhile, adjusted EBITDA expanded 570bps y/y to $11.0 million:

Valuation and key takeaways

To a large extent, however, much of The RealReal's strength is already priced into its stock, even after the post-earnings dip. At current share prices just under $7, the company trades at a market cap of $717.4 million. After we net off the $172.2 million of cash and $303.5 million of debt on The RealReal's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $848.7 million.

Meanwhile, for the current fiscal year FY25, The RealReal has guided to $645-$660 million in revenue, representing 7-10% y/y revenue growth, alongside similar GMV growth rates. It's also pointing to $25 million in midpoint adjusted EBITDA, which represents a near-tripling of adjusted EBITDA on a nominal basis, and a 3.8% margin (or 220bps of expansion this year).

The RealReal trades at 34x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA. Of course, the company is still posting nascent profitability and improvement in sales/GMV trends (potentially driven by the company's better salesforce retention) could help the company achieve faster adjusted EBITDA expansion.

For now, however, The RealReal's ~30x multiple against its official outlook already suggests plenty of valuation cushion for outperformance. I'd wait for The RealReal to retreat to levels around $4.50-$5.00 (representing ~25x adjusted EBITDA) before considering buying back in.

data.title: My No. 1 Priority Stock: Why Nvidia's 52% Growth Is Just The Start
data.description:
Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) is my #1 priority stock in my personal portfolio for 2025.

Actually, if you include ETFs, my allocation to Nvidia is even higher.

I love Nvidia for so many reasons, including the three that I'll explain today.

The fundamentals of my equity portfolio.

According to Morningstar's analyst consensus, 44% of my portfolio consists of equities with 29% 5-year earnings growth and a very nice 4% yield.

  • SCHD has just 3.5% with 6% growth.

My portfolio's valuation is a very reasonable 18.3X, while the returns on assets and equity (quality metrics) are 50% to 100% better than the S&P average.

In other words, world-class blue chips (Ultra SWANs, in fact) are growing like weeds, paying rich and low-risk dividends, and trading at a PEGY ratio of 0.55.

  • S&P 1.42 (and it's 21% overvalued unless earnings growth is at 11.8% as is currently priced in).

In other words, my portfolio's valuation is 61% cheaper than the S&P 500's when adjusted for yield and growth, thanks to my selection of the best companies for yield, quality, and growth.

  • It's an even better bargain when you consider the 46% better returns on equity and 97% better returns on assets.

How Confident Am I in My Nvidia Position? 1500 Shares Ahead Of Earnings This Quarter!

In 2025 alone, I bought Nvidia seven times before the February 26th earnings report (Wednesday after the bell).

In other words, next week is this earnings season's most important earnings report, as Nvidia is the unofficial captain of the AI tech boom.

It's not just the second-most valuable company in the world, but it will likely set the tone for the next three months if it beats and raises (as it has every quarter since ChatGPT came out in November 2022) or misses and somehow disappoints.

Let me show you the three reasons why I'm confident that Nvidia will beat and raise (like usual). If it disappoints, I'm ready to back up the truck with limit orders to buy more.

I Have Almost $400K Worth Of Limits Set On Nvidia

I expect Nvidia to increase after earnings because it has historically beat EPS by 11% (last four quarters) and remains supply-constrained.

But I reset my limits every quarter (before earnings) to keep Nvidia at my 12.5% max risk cap target allocation.

In other words, if NVDA falls 10%, it falls to 12% of my portfolio, and my limits are programmed to bring it back to 12.5%.

The more Nvidia falls, the more I buy, always bringing it back to 12.5%.

So when Nvidia bottoms and recovers, I thoroughly benefit from its incredible growth.

Last year, this "dynamic rebalancing" increased the gains in my portfolio by 38% (NVDA limit buying contributed 25% to that boost).

Ok, I'm very bullish on Nvidia, including buying 1,500 shares this quarter (a 33% increase in my biggest position).

Now let me show you why.

Reason 1: Has Exceptional Growth Potential For The Next Two Years

Nvidia is a company growing at over 30%, yet it trades at a 22% discount (actually 26%, as I'll explain in reason three).

  • Based on the PEGY ratio.

The growth consensus going into earnings is remarkable.

  • 52% EPS growth in 2025.
  • 25% growth in 2026.

But let me show you why that might be wildly underestimating what NVDA might deliver this year.

In the last year NVDA beat estimates by an average of 11%.

That would be 63% EPS growth if that is sustained.

And for this particular quarter? 64% growth consensus and 75% historical beat expectation.

That's remarkable.

But let me show you why NVDA might beat expectations by its historical 11% this year and possibly by 35% next year.

Nvidia has been supply constrained for years, and things have improved recently.

During 2023, NVDA was constrained by TSMC wager capacity, but Nvidia's CEO noted wafer supply itself is not the bottleneck anymore.

So what is? CoWoS packaging is the bottleneck right now.

CoWoS (Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate) combines multiple chips into a single powerful unit.

This is the example of Grace Blackwell, where multiple GPUs are packaged with various interlink technologies and sold as multi-million dollar systems.

TSMC recently announced its CoWoS capacity upgrade plans.

TSMC has announced it will expand CoWoS advanced packaging capacity by over 60% CAGR through 2026.

If NVDA remains supply constrained this year and next year, then its theoretical sales and EPS growth is 60% this year and 60% next year.

That's 156% potential EPS growth courtesy of 160% CoWoS capacity increase from TSMC.

What is the FactSet consensus? 90% EPS growth.

Let me repeat that. In 2025 and 2026 NVDA is expected to almost double EPS (and free cash flow).

But according to management, the #1 limiting factor is CoWoS and TSMC is planning on increasing CoWoS capacity by 156%.

That's 66% higher than current expectation.

Does that mean that NVDA will beat expectations by 66% over the next eight quarters?

  • 6.54% beat per quarter.

No, it means that it could, and do you think it's priced for that? Do you think there is any reasonable way that NVDA grows earnings by 156% in the next two years and investors end up disappointed?

How much multiple compression is possible? For two reasons I think very little is likely, and now I'll explain why.

Reason 2: Nvidia Is Becoming A GPU Utility With Subscription-Based Sales

Tom Lee of Fundastrat has the most optimistic bull-case long-term forecast on Nvidia, "10X in 10 years".

That might sound outlandish, but it actually represents 25% CAGR returns for 10 years.

That's compared to management's guidance of "20% to 25% in AI chip demand growth for the foreseeable future," extending the foreseeable future to 10 years (2035).

But wait a second. Isn't that about $35 trillion in market cap? Yes, it is, but let me explain why Nvidia's historical 40X PE could persist for the next decade if it can maintain the 20% to 25% growth management is discussing "for the foreseeable future."

Nvidia is transitioning from a cyclical hardware company (like Apple was) to a tech utility.

Apple used to trade at low double-digit P/Es until it became a consumer staple with steadily more stable, recurring revenue.

NVDA is not only offering software subscriptions ($4,500 per GPU) to maximize the productivity (and profitability) of its hardware (with 110% CAGR management growth guidance through 2030).

  • $30 billion in AI software subscriptions by 2030 vs $1 billion run rate by end of 2023 and $2 billion by end of 2024.

This isn't going to represent the majority of sales (like at Broadcom) anytime soon, even in 2030 it's likely to be less than 10% (per management guidance).

  • Some analysts expect $600 billion in sales by 2030, which is almost 3X today.

Phil Panaro is the analyst from Boston Consulting Group whose offering that $600 billion bullish sales forecast for NVDA in 2030, based on NVDA maintaining its GPU and AI software (CUDA) lead over time.

He expects that in addition to GPUs, NVDA is going to be selling integrated systems that will be able to generate 40% annual growth in overall AI hardware sales.

Already robotics and cars are generating 70% growth for NVDA and in 2026 Jensen Huang thinks humanoid robots are going to get their ChatGPT moment.

In other words, AI driven humanoid robots will bring AI into the physical world which represents about 95% of the global economy.

  • Intelligence (software based stuff) is 5% of GDP.

In other words, the AI revolution hasn't even begun to get started, we're potentially looking at 20X the addressable market starting next year.

And in the short-terms, despite plenty of risks to what might go wrong with fundamentals, the facts are that 52% EPS growth in 2025 and 25% growth in 2026 is plenty of catalysts for strong returns even if the best-case scenario doesn't occur.

Reason Three: Nvidia Is A Buffett-Style "Fat Pitch"

Nvidia's median and average PEGY ratio is 0.9. What is it today?

  • Blended 12-Month Growth Rate: 48.30%

  • PEGY Ratio: 0.66

That's a 26% historical discount based on the PEGY ratio.

Justified Return Potential=48.30% growth +34.81% upside to fair value= 83.11% 12-month fundamentally justified return potential.

NOT A FORECAST. This means that "IF AND ONLY IF NVDA grows as expected and returns to historical fair value PEGY ratio, then an 83% increase in the next 12 months is 100% justified by fundamentals".

If NVDA grows EPS by 48% in the next 12 months, returns to fair value, and rises 83%, I can guarantee you most people will be screaming, "BUBBLE!"

I'm telling you now and showing you the math to prove those people wrong and keep you from selling one of the greatest growth stories in history unnecessarily.

Risk Profile: Why Nvidia Isn't Right For Everyone

Let me be very clear that Nvidia's fundamentals, as incredible as they look today, might rapidly deteriorate if worst-case scenarios happen.

For example, the Trump administration has discussed 25% tariffs on chips from non-American companies (like TSMC).

That would directly impact Nvidia and hurt margins, which analysts and investors are concerned about. It might go into effect as early as April 2nd.

  • I consider it a low-probability risk, given that Fabs take 3 to 4 years to build, and US chip makers can't replace TSMC chips anytime soon.
  • TSMC makes 90% of the most advanced chips US companies buy.
  • The US is in an AGI race with China, and this would be unnecessarily shooting ourselves in the foot, and no tariffs (not even 100%) can possibly quicken the pace of fab construction.

However, as seen with the DeepSeek freakout and initial tariff freakouts (one-day flash crashes), Nvidia can plunge far more than the broader market.

This year the S&P's largest peak decline is 3.06%, not even a pullback.

Nvidia? It's still in a 20% bear market!

In the last year, NVDA's peak intra-day decline was -35% (over three weeks culminating in the 16% decline on August 5th, 2024, Japan Yen Carry trade collapse).

Nvidia's bear markets are crashes, with the company nearly being cut in half in its worst declines.

That's not a bug; it's a feature! That's why limit buys on Nvidia are so effective.

When the world loses sight of fundamentals, reacts to headlines, and algos are desperate to sell (at any price), I'm right there to provide liquidity.

  • This is what "being greedy when others are fearful" is all about.
  • We're the buyers of last resort, and why we make so much money when our companies recover.

The biggest fundamental risk to my thesis? That the AI productivity boom turns out to be overblown.

If the actual productivity boost from AI is on the low end of Goldman's analysis, then the "GPU utility" thesis for NVDA could go away.

And if that happens NVDA is still a cyclical hyper-volatile growth company.

Nvidia's EPS growth over the last 20 years has been incredible.

And yet, the median growth rate is negative!

It can see earnings fall as much as 82% in a single year (Great Recession), but even in 2022 (not a recession year), EPS fell 25%, and NVDA fell 65% in that bear market.

In other words, it's easy to "buy the dip" and buy with confidence when the fundamentals are booming, like in 2025 and likely 2026.

But if consensus estimates dive?

NVDA's growth outlook has ranged from 15% to almost 45% in the last few years alone.

And if this chart went back to 1999, You'd likely have seen some pessimistic growth estimates!

In other words, when you own a company, even world-beater blue-chips and Ultra SWANs like Nvidia, you have to remember that there is a lot of faith in management involved.

Most companies that fall 70% or more never recover.

Nvidia has fallen that much twice.

During those times, the fundamental outlook looked much worse than it is today.

Today, Nvidia looks like God's own growth stock. However, I guarantee that will not be the case in the future, even if it does achieve Tom Lee's "10X in 10 years" bull case forecast proves correct.

Bottom Line: NVDA Is 26% Undervalued Vs. Its Historical PEGY Ratio With 52% Consensus Growth And 100% Potential Growth This Year (And 25% to 50% Next Year)

Nvidia isn't going to double its earnings every year, but it might do so this year, and up to 50% next year, if TSMC's scaling plans are successful and NVDA remains supply constrained.

Even if the growth consensus is merely correct (and it doesn't beat expectations like it has by 11% in the last year) that's 52% growth this year and 25% next year, for a company trading at 32X forward earnings.

A 0.66 PEGY ratio right now is far better than the S&P's 1.42 and shows how NVDA is a potentially explosive Buffett-style "fat pitch".

One whose wild volatility is a feature, not a bug, for savvy investors who understand that narratives can shift overnight (DeepSeek Monday crash) but fundamentals usually don't.

I'm not saying "go all in" on NVDA. That would be wildly reckless.

I'm targeting 12.5% max overweight on NVDA and would never recommend anyone go more than 22% into any Ultra SWAN, no matter their confidence or how strong the fundamentals look today.

But whatever your risk profile and optimal allocation for Nvidia, buying before earnings, at a 21% to 26% discount is a very smart move.

I can say that with 80% statistical confidence, that given the best available data we have today, anyone buying NVDA right now will look like a genius in 5 to 10 years.

Just be ready for the wild ride between now and then.

data.title: Pagaya: Severely Undervalued Despite Improving Business Performance
data.description:
In May last year, I initiated my coverage on Pagaya Technologies Ltd. (NASDAQ:PGY) with a buy rating thanks to the robust growth of its network volume, improving bottom line performance, and its efforts to reduce risk exposure. Since then, the stock is up 34% on the back of the company’s latest Q4 earnings where it reported record network volume, revenues, FRLPC margin, and adjusted EBITDA.

With the company taking a final impairment charge on its vintage 2021-2023 loan portfolio, Pagaya expects to achieve its first GAAP profitable quarter in Q2 2025 and achieve its first full-year of GAAP profitability in FY 2025. With that in mind, I believe the company has the potential to grow its profitability substantially over the coming years due to its efforts to reduce its risk exposure. As is, I expect the company to exit FY 2025 with a risk retention rate near 3%, a substantial improvement from 6% in Q4 2024.

Moreover, the company is testing new products with its existing partners, which could be a tailwind to revenues in the coming years as the testing process is still in its early stages. In light of these factors, I’m reiterating my buy rating for Pagaya and raising my price target to $57 per share, implying 253% upside from current levels.

Q4 Overview

In Q4, Pagaya reported YoY revenue growth of 28% from $218 million to $279.4 million, driven by fee revenues growing 31% over the same period from $210.4 million to $275.7 million. Pagaya’s topline growth was a direct result of network volume growing 9% YoY during Q4 from $2.4 billion to $2.6 billion in addition to its take rate increasing YoY from 8.8% to 10.6%. In my opinion, the increase in Pagaya’s take rate is a testament to the quality of its product as it indicates that its partners are willing to pay more to use its services.

The increase in Pagaya’s take rate contributed to fee revenues less production costs (FRLPC) growing from $75.9 million in the prior year at an FRLPC margin of 3.2% to $117.5 million at a record FRLPC margin of 4.5%. In light of the topline growth and the FRLPC margin expansion, Pagaya generated a record operating income of $31.7 million at an operating margin of 11.4% in Q4 2024 compared to an operating income of $10.7 million at a 4.9% margin in the prior year.

As such, Pagaya generated a record adjusted EBITDA of $64.2 million at a margin of 23% in Q4 2024 compared to $34.2 million at a margin of 15.7% in the prior year.

Moving on to Pagaya’s balance sheet, the company exited Q4 with a cash balance of $195.7 million and held $756.3 million of investments in loans and securities. Meanwhile, the company has total debt of $674.4 million, of which $516.9 million are long-term debt.

Looking forward, Pagaya is guiding Q1 2025 network volume between $2.5 to $2.7 billion, total revenues between $280 to $295 million, adjusted EBITDA between $65 to $75 million, and a GAAP net income of -$20 million to breakeven. For the full-year, the company expects network volume between $10.25 to $11.75 billion, total revenues between $1.15 to $1.275 billion, adjusted EBITDA between $265 to $315 million, and a GAAP net income between -$10 million to $40 million.

Expensive Lesson Is Yielding Better Results

Financial performance aside, Pagaya’s Q4 earnings included a substantial $235 million impairment loss on poor performing loans spanning 2021 through 2023. By taking this loss, Pagaya believes that it has taken the majority of potential impairment charges for the 2021-2023 vintages, which is expected to boost earnings in FY 2025 and beyond.

As is, the vast majority of loans Pagaya holds on its balance sheet are 2024 vintages which now comprise 68% of the total loans on its balance sheet, while 2023 vintages represent 20% of its total held loans and 2022 and prior vintages represent 12% of total loans on Pagaya’s balance sheet.

These 2024 vintages are performing better than prior vintages, as in personal loans, delinquency rates are stable around 1%, while in auto loans, delinquencies are in a downward trajectory, as shown in the following 2 graphs.

In addition to helping boost profitability, the impairment loss Pagaya took on its past poor performing loans has contributed to improved underwriting, as shown by the performance of the 2024 vintages. In fact, the lessons learned from the impaired loans, albeit an expensive one, has made management extremely conservative in giving out loans, in my opinion, as the company continues to convert less than 1% of loan applications evaluated by its platform.

With that in mind, it should be noted that the conversion rate in Q4 2024 was at its highest since Q1 2023, indicating that Pagaya’s partners are increasing their risk appetite in the current macro conditions. This could be a tailwind to future network volume growth, and in turn fee revenues, especially if Pagaya’s take rate continues to increase.

Diversified Funding Sources To Improve Risk Exposure

Another reason I’m bullish on Pagaya is its continuous efforts to diversify its funding sources. In Q4 2024, Pagaya’s network volume was $2.6 billion and it purchased loans worth $155.2 million, indicating a risk retention rate of about 6%. This figure is significantly down from the 10.8% reported in Q1 2024, at the time of my previous coverage.

In the Q4 earnings call, management stated that they expect non-ABS funding sources to contribute 25-50% of its total funding in FY 2025. Meanwhile, the company has access to $12-$13 billion in funding, of which $6-7 billion are in ABS funding, $3 billion in AUM at privately managed funds, $1 billion in pass-through securitizations, and $2 billion in forward flow agreements.

Accordingly, the company can fund up to 52% of its network volume through ABS issuances, at the midpoint, which carry a risk retention rate of at least 5%, as required by Dodd-Frank, while simultaneously fund up to 48% of network volume through non-ABS sources which carry a risk retention rate between 0-1%. In this way, Pagaya has the potential to exit FY 2025 with a net risk retention rate near 3%, which would be a significant improvement from the 6% rate reported in Q4 2024.

By shifting more risk to its funding partners, Pagaya stands to reduce its exposure to future impairment losses, in case its loans perform badly, which in turn will have a positive impact on future earnings. In addition, reducing its risk exposure has led Pagaya’s management to state that the company won’t have to dilute shares anytime soon, which could help alleviate some concerns regarding the company’s history of constantly diluting its shares.

New Products & Monetizing Existing Relationships

Pagaya also has the potential to further grow its topline and expand its FRLPC margin through the introduction of new products to better monetize its existing relationships. Management shared in the Q4 earnings call that the company is testing a pre-screen product with 3 existing partners, and early signs show that this product could be a major boon for Pagaya’s future topline growth. This product leverages Pagaya’s access to lending partners’ customer data to provide recommendations for additional products to customers who deserve them.

During the testing phase, the pre-screen product has allowed Pagaya’s partners to increase engagement at a significantly lower CAC. Accordingly, this product could help Pagaya’s partners to grow their businesses without any additional marketing costs, which could help Pagaya increase its take rate in the future.

At the same time, Pagaya is testing affiliate expansion with existing partners to help them acquire new customers, driving customer and revenue growth for these partners. As these products prove to be beneficial for Pagaya’s customers, the company’s pricing power could increase, especially since its new products don’t require any increase in its partners’ marketing costs. In this way, Pagaya’s FRLPC has the potential to continue increasing in the coming years, boosting profitability in turn.

Valuation

At the midpoint of management’s adjusted EBITDA guidance, $290 million, Pagaya is trading at a forward EV/adjusted EBITDA multiple of 5.77. This indicates that Pagaya may be severely undervalued relative to the sector median of 16.17. By applying the sector median EV/EBITDA multiple, my price target for Pagaya is $56.64, implying 253% upside from its current valuation.

Risks

The main risk to my bullish thesis on Pagaya is an economic slowdown, potentially leading to a recession. Even with improved underwriting, an economic downturn could lead to higher delinquency and default rates across Pagaya’s loan portfolio. This could increase future impairment charges, and potentially see the company struggle to fund its network volume through its partners.

An economic downturn could also impact the accuracy of Pagaya’s AI models since they would make previous data irrelevant, which could see the company be in a similar situation as it was last year, where a significant portion of its loans perform badly.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I remain bullish on Pagaya, especially following its Q4 earnings. The company continues to grow its network volume at an impressive rate and grow its take rate and FRLPC margin, a testament to the quality of its product. The company is also making strides in reducing its risk exposure by diversifying its funding sources away from ABS issuances, which require a high risk retention rate of 5%. The lower risk exposure could see Pagaya exit FY 2025 with a risk retention rate near 3%, a substantial improvement from the 6% reported in Q4 2024.

Meanwhile, Pagaya is testing new products with existing partners to better monetize its partner network, and these products are showing promising early signs of helping its partners grow their customers and revenues without any increase in marketing costs. Once these products are proven, I expect Pagaya’s take rate to expand substantially, which will reflect on its profitability in the future. It is for these reasons that I’m reiterating my buy rating for Pagaya and raising my price target to $57 per share, representing 253% upside from current levels.

data.title: Aurora Cannabis: This Phoenix Has Risen From The Ashes (Strong Buy Initiation)
data.description:
The global cannabis industry has had quite an interesting few years.

After seeing stock prices run wild in the speculative markets of the late 2010s, shares have since plummeted on the back of woeful financial results across the sector and a lack of investor interest.

As U.S. legalization continues to get kicked down the road, you'd be forgiven for thinking that the majority of the industry has been permanently left for dead. Many companies in the cannabis business now trade, to this day, down 95% or more from highs, as prospects have dimmed across the board.

However, something interesting has been happening over the last few quarters.

Aurora Cannabis (NASDAQ:ACB), one of the market darlings of the last hype cycle, has begun to put out some truly excellent financial results.

While the stock price remains moribund, upon closer inspection, the company has begun to right the ship.

Terrible margins, a lack of differentiation, and intense competition have been replaced by excellent gross margins, a strong position in a tightly regulated global market, and an incredibly robust balance sheet with almost no debt.

While there's still a way to go in terms of building out a long-term track record, we think ACB may have what it takes to rise from the ashes and produce excellent total returns from here on out for shareholders.

Today, we'll be breaking down ACB's recent results, giving our outlook for the stock, and explaining why we're so bullish on this small cap growth play.

Sound good? Let's dive in.

ACB's Financials

If you're anything like us, then you're likely of the opinion that the financials of the global cannabis business don't make a lot of sense.

At least, they didn't - the last time we looked at them in 2021.

At that time, as we just mentioned, the market for recreational cannabis was rammed with competition. The market opportunity was huge, which got investors excited, but this excitement about growth covered up sub-par unit economics on the part of the market's favorite stocks, including ACB.

With negative operating margins and a lack of 'ways out', we thought that the industry would need a long maturation process before it became investible.

As it turns out, we were right.

Now, four years on, for all intents and purposes, ACB - in particular - has completely righted the ship.

After making the strategic decision to focus on the global medical market (and stop competing as aggressively in Canada's recreational market), the firm has built out a truly impressive global product portfolio in Canada, Australia, Germany, the UK, Poland, and elsewhere.

Now, on a TTM basis, the company produces roughly $223 million in revenue. More than 70% of this is from medical cannabis products, with the remainder coming from ACB's plant propagation and recreational cannabis segments.

At the same time, ACB's stake in Bevo has allowed the firm to add needed stability to the financial profile, build efficiencies, and drastically reduce costs, especially at the COGS level, which has led to a significantly strengthening gross margin profile for the business writ large:

In mid-2022, ACB had gross margins hovering in the 0-10% range. However, as time has gone on and ACB management has continued to execute and focus on GMP for the EU and Oceania, costs have come down on a nominal basis, while revenues have gone up, leading to extremely impressive GM% expansion:

Now, GM%'s stand at roughly 66% TTM, which is a marked turnaround for the business.

It's really hard to overstate how impressive this is.

With gross margin improvements like that, ACB has transformed the prospects of the company, from a loss-making loser into a firm that has a lot more control over long-term planning and execution.

When you boost gross margins from ~flat (or even negative) to 66%, while continuing to increase revenues, it changes everything and allows for the company to begin retaining profits on behalf of shareholders.

But how was this achieved?

In short, by focusing on Medical, ACB has been able to grow margins and market share in these key markets. Less competition and higher end-market prices have allowed ACB to focus on quality, not quantity, and build out a robust manufacturing base capable of competing over the long run.

Recently what really excites us is ACB's TTM operating profit, which just came into the black for the first time ever:

This shows that ACB is now capable of standing on its' own 'two feet', and that more substantial profits are likely on the way.

At the same time, the company has also managed to slim down bloat and debt by divesting into a much sleeker balance sheet. Now, with basically no debt, and tons of cash, PPE, and inventory, we think ACB is extremely well positioned to grow market share and financials further.

Looking forward, on the recent call, management mentioned that they saw favorable pricing conditions for the coming quarters, which should sustain ACB's GM's and positive EBITDA going forward:

So we generally do see higher margins in our medical and especially the international market. So as more and more of our revenue has shifted or has grown in those sectors, that overall contributes to the increased margins we're seeing, not only through the medical cannabis channel, but then implications, total company as well. And we're also seeing operational efficiencies, which has an impact on our cost of goods sold. So that combination of favorable prices as well as managed cost is contributing to our margin here. And we -- to one of your second part of your question, we do expect these margins to continue to stay favorable in the coming quarters...

Broadly, now that ACB's margins have been structurally refined and improved, we see ACB's growth coming from a few main areas, including deepening sales within established markets, new markets that de-schedule cannabis, and the call option of broad legalization of global recreational markets.

Taken together, these growth drivers, when combined with ACB's much more resilient margin profile and increased business focus / synergy, should produce solid top and bottom line results for investors.

ACB's Valuation

So - how expensive is the stock? If the market is already pricing in ACB's recent progress, then it could reduce the attractiveness of an investment into the shares.

Thankfully, right now, we think ACB is trading for an absolute song.

This is a quick overview of where things currently sit

  • $223 million in TTM revenue
  • $42.9 million in TTM EBITDA
  • $3.3 million in long term debt
  • $426 million in equity
  • $318 million in Market Cap

This puts ACB's multiple at the following levels:

  • 1.42x sales
  • 7.4x EBITDA
  • 0.74x book

In our view, for a high margin, growing, consumer business with strong market share and limited competition, this appears to be a very, very attractive price point.

From a different angle, if you believe analyst estimates, then you're looking at a rapidly shrinking P/E that gets into the mid-single digits by the end of the decade:

Given that we think the multiple has a good chance of expanding from this price point as the market comes to better understand ACB's true financial picture, we think that ACB could trade up to 10x EBITDA by 2026.

If revenues grow 15% YoY over the NTM, GM%'s solidify at 70%, and SG&A remains flat at around $100 million per year, then you're looking at roughly $79 million in operating profit and a similar EBITDA figure.

If the company executes and the multiple expands to 10x, then you're looking at $790 million in market cap, more than a 'double' for current shareholders.

In our view, not a lot of 'new' things need to happen for ACB to achieve this, the company simply needs to keep executing, and if the multiple expands on improved market understanding of ACB's prospects, then we could see growth to 10x EBITDA or higher.

Even without expansion, if ACB can grow EBITDA towards 79 million over the NTM, then you're looking at a market cap of ~580 million, more than 80% higher than where we're currently trading.

Either way, we think ACB is set up to produce very strong total returns over both the short run and long run.

Risks

That said, there are some risks associated with our thesis.

First, if ACB's competition heats up in key markets like Germany or Australia, then we'd expect a pricing pressure which could hurt gross margins, and thus, EBITDA and other profitability metrics.

This, in turn, could dent the share price.

Right now, the company is relatively insulated from the competitive bloodbath happening in the recreational space as medical products need to meet strict GMP requirements, which takes extra capital, know-how, and upfront cost to navigate.

However, if this changes, then we could see lower share prices.

Additionally, ACB continues to dilute shareholders at a fast clip:

This is a clear negative, and it could wipe out gains in top and bottom-line results if EPS is driven down by an increasing denominator.

We're hopeful that the company's newfound profitability and positive cash from operations may reduce the need for ACB to dilute, but it's still a risk moving forward.

Summary

Overall, though, we see ACB's market position, product portfolio, and recent financial transformation as being incredibly bullish for both short- and long-term investors. Now, it's just a matter of waiting for management to continue executing on the opportunity as the market (hopefully) begins to realize what is happening.

There are risks, and the stock remains volatile, which means that this isn't a 'safe', or 'Blue Chip' play.

However, given the potential upside, we're forced to initiate coverage on ACB with a 'Strong Buy' rating.

Stay safe out there!

data.title: BDC Weekly Review: Few Worries About MSDL Share Cap Raise
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company ("BDC") sector from both the bottom-up - highlighting individual news and events - as well as the top-down - providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the second week of February.

Market Action

BDCs had a very strong week with a 2% gain, only below that of mREITS in the broader income market. Solid earnings and hot inflation numbers have supported the sector. CGBD underperformed as its rating was cut at BOA due to an upcoming merger with a private affiliate BDC - see the discussion below. Month-to-date, the sector is up about 1%.

The average valuation in our coverage is trading at the top end of its range over the past decade or so.

Market Themes

The Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL) is asking shareholders to approve an amendment to its certificate of incorporation to increase the number of shares from 101m to 501m (common shares would increase from 100m to 500m with the stub being for preferreds). The company currently has 89m and 83m of shares issued and outstanding.

That might seem like a big increase in shares however many BDCs are prolific issuers with no ill effect. Moreover, it’s not like MSDL is going to lift its share count to that level anytime soon - it’s just they don’t want to come back to shareholders for small increases every year or two.

For example CSWC shares outstanding grew from 18m in 2020 to around 50m today, a nearly 3x increase and no one really is too worried about CSWC issuing too many shares - the stock trades at a 40% premium.

There are dilution concerns when issuing new stock however this is more of a concern for traditional "widget" companies rather than BDCs which are basically portfolios of securities. This is because it is hard for "widget makers" to swiftly increase production and find new customers so new share issuance is typically dilutive of earnings.

The reason there are no dilution concerns for BDCs is that the new capital received from share issuance can be put to work fairly quickly - BDC portfolio turnover is often in the double digits per quarter with frequent repayments and new investments. That capital can also be leveraged quickly using credit facilities or even bond issuance. New share issuance can also boost returns as we have seen with CSWC using at-the-market offerings above the NAV. It is very unlikely MSDL would issue shares below the NAV as it’s not something BDCs do often.

Market Commentary

Carlyle Secured Lending (CGBD) tumbled 4% due to a cut at BOA to Underperform from Buy on expectation of underperformance following the completion of its merger with affiliated private BDC CSL3. The view seems to be that the merger will unlock CSL3 private shares and allow those investors to sell them in the public market, depressing the price of CGBD. That’s certainly a likelihood - we have seen similar price dips of BDCs that IPO’d last year.

However, prior to the cut the stock was trading at a nearly 10% premium and near its highest relative valuation vs. the sector. On both counts, this is expensive for its historic return profile so in our view it’s odd to have a Buy rating irrespective of what’s happening with the merger.

A sweetener for CGBD investors is the exchange of the CGBD private preferred held by a Carlyle affiliate which could have a dilution overhang of 5% of CGBD NAV if converted. Instead, the preferred will be exchanged at the NAV. It’s not much of a sweetener however since management always insisted the preferred would not be converted and the stock works as fairly cheap equity (cheaper than the common). There are some fixed cost ($2.5m) savings as well to be had. The overlap in assets appears to be 100%. It’s not clear what the interest expense of CSL3 is, however as a private BDC it would likely have a higher interest expense which creates a small headwind for CGBD.

Hercules Capital (HTGC) delivered very strong results with a total NAV return of 6.8% for the quarter. Net investment income fell around 5% to $0.492 which explains the cut in the supplemental. Total dividend is a penny below net investment income and could see further cuts as investment income should fall next quarter as well, under pressure from lower SOFR.

Non-accruals remained low at 0.5%, but there was a net realized loss of 1.7% of equity. Overall, HTGC remains an impressive BDC with a 21% total NAV return over the last year (only second to MAIN) with an equally impressive premium of 81%. That premium leaves little on the table for investors as the valuation adjusted total NAV return is only a bit above the median level, leaving the stock pretty much priced for perfection.

The earnings picture of the BDC sector is, so far, a pretty good one with limited credit distress, only slightly lower earnings and stable NAV behavior. Portfolio quality has likely been boosted by an improvement in borrower interest coverage which should limit realized losses and unrealized depreciation at a modest cost of lower investment income.

On the downside, however, is the fact that valuations have had a strong run-up and offer limited opportunity for new holdings, outside of starter positions. We have seen that especially with Fidus Investment Corp (FDUS) which has delivered a nearly 40% return over the past year, causing us to lower our allocation in favor of Nuveen Churchill Direct Lending (NCDL).

data.title: Decomposition Of U.S. Equity Returns Over Time - Is It Different This Time?
data.description:
By Indrani De, CFA, PRM, Head of Global Investment Research, Americas, FTSE Russell | Indhu Raghavan, CFA, Manager, Global Investment Research, FTSE Russell

US equity return decomposition shows that the large US equity rallies in 2023 and 2024 were driven mainly by valuation expansion, unlike what we have seen during market recoveries in previous business cycles when, after an initial bounce in valuation, earnings growth was the main driver of total return. The analysis also highlights a structural shift post-2010, when US equities hugely outperformed global peers, where valuation changes played a far bigger role in US equity returns than during 2003-10. This suggests that US equity market returns could be more susceptible to changes in investor sentiment and that investors could potentially face greater equity market volatility going forward.

The US stock market recorded a second year of stellar double-digit growth in 2024. What can investors expect going forward?

A lot depends on the drivers of an equity rally, given that equity returns are driven by three main components - earnings growth, valuation changes and dividend yield. We analyze time series data of the valuation change, earnings growth and dividend yield components of US equity returns over the last 22 years. We look at how their relative contributions have evolved over time, and the pattern of these components at similar points in the economic cycle (e.g. years after recession, or recession fears on the back of monetary tightening.) This helps to think about the sustainability of these returns and the risk factors involved.

Exhibit 1 shows the annual total return for the US large-cap Russell 1000 index with a breakdown of contributions to that total return from price-to-earnings (PE) multiple expansion, earnings growth and dividend yield.

Methodology: We take the price index and IBES 12-month forward PE at any given point in time to calculate the implied earnings per share for the index. We use the values from 31 December of each year for PE and EPS to then calculate each component's growth rate from the year before. The dividend yield is calculated as the difference between index total return and price return for that year.

Exhibit 1: Russell 1000 total return decomposition, 12M, USD, Percent

The Russell 1000 index returned 26.5% and 24.5% in 2023 and 2024, respectively. Of the 26.5% total return in 2023, 19.4% pts (or nearly three-quarters) came from valuation expansion and 5.4% pts (or just one-fifth) from earnings growth. In 2024, 12.5% pts (or just over half of total return) came from valuation expansion and 10.5% pts (just over two-fifth) from earnings growth. The stock rally of the last two years also followed a massive pullback in equities in 2022 (-19.1% total return for Russell 1000), when the US Fed started aggressively tightening monetary conditions in response to spiralling inflation, sparking fears of a recession, and valuations contracted massively. In 2022, PE multiple contraction shaved off 24.5% pts from total return, while earnings growth of 4.0% and dividend yield of 1.4% softened the extent of the drawdown.

While the US avoided a recession during the subsequent two years after the monetary tightening that started in 2022, earnings growth was not the main driver of returns. How does this compare to history?

Is the current rally different from those in previous business cycles?

It is worth looking at two time periods when the US stock market recovered from sharp pullbacks-after the bursting of the Tech bubble during 2000-02[1] and the Global Financial Crisis during 2008-09.

During 2003-05, earnings growth contributed more to stock market returns than multiple expansion. In fact, multiples bounced in 2003 and then contracted subsequently in 2004 and 2005. In 2003, a total return of 29.9% was driven by 18.0% pts (or nearly two-thirds) of earnings growth, 9.8% pts of valuation expansion and 2.1% pts in dividend yield. In 2004 and 2005, earnings growth contributed 15.9% pts and 12.9% pts, respectively, to total return, which was about two-thirds and just over half of that return. At the same time, multiples contracted in 2004 and 2005.

We see a similar recovery during 2009-11 when multiples bounced back sharply in 2009, and earnings growth led stock market returns in the subsequent two years. In 2009, a total return of 28.4% was driven entirely by valuation expansion of 29.8% pts, while earnings contracted by 4.0% pts and dividend yield contributed 2.6% pts. In 2010 and 2011, earnings growth contributed 23.5% pts and 12.6% pts, respectively, to total return, which was about two-thirds and just under half of that return. Once again, multiples contracted during 2010-11.

The stock market recovery of 2023-24 seemed to follow a similar pattern initially-with a strong bounce back in multiples in 2023 and a more gradual recovery in earnings. Thereafter, in 2024, things looked different this time. It is hard to miss that the level of earnings growth this time around is much lower, and multiple expansion continued to contribute just over half of returns even in 2024.

These numbers may reflect both the narrowness of the US equity rally in the most recent period and the degree to which it was driven by sentiment. US returns have been dominated by the US Technology industry on the back of AI-fuelled optimism.

We do the same analysis for small caps to see if things look different for them relative to large caps. Looking at the small-cap space offers another perspective.

Exhibit 2 shows the annual total return for the Russell 2000 index with the breakdown of contributions from multiple expansion, earnings growth and dividend yield.

Exhibit 2: Russell 2000 total return decomposition, 12M, USD, Percent

The Russell 2000 small-cap index returned 16.9% and 11.5% over 2023 and 2024, respectively. This growth came primarily from multiple expansion of 19.7% pts and 8.2% pts, respectively, in 2023 and 2024, or over two-thirds of total return in both years. The contrast of this breakdown with the previous two time periods is starker. We see that during the 2003-05 recovery, it was earnings growth that dominated stock market returns in all the three years, and during 2009-11, earnings growth led the way in 2010 and 2011. The results for Russell 2000 are consistent with or even stronger than Russell 1000, i.e. market recoveries began with valuation expansion to be quickly followed by earnings growth as the main driver of returns, and the pattern of returns in 2023-24, of being led primarily by valuation expansion, is starkly different from past cycles.

US small caps are much more sensitive to domestic economic and monetary conditions than large caps. And small-cap earnings have recovered much less so than in the large-cap space and relative to history. It reinforces the idea that the stock market growth we have seen over the last two years in the US has been carried more by positive sentiment-led valuation expansion than a recovery in earnings compared to past cycles.

The expanding role of valuation expansion

In fact, if we look at the 14 years after the height of the European debt crisis in 2010, when US equity outperformance of global peers began in earnest,[2] we see that valuation expansion has played an increasing role in driving total returns in the US during 2011-24. The Russell 1000 index recorded a positive total return in 12 of those 14 years. Multiple expansion dominated returns in seven out of those 12 years, contributing between 50% and 90% of total return. By contrast, during 2003-10, when Russell 1000 had positive total returns in seven out of the eight years, only in one of those eight positive-return years was valuation expansion the primary driver for total returns.

Exhibit 3 shows the annualised total return for the Russell 1000 index during 2011-24 and 2003-10 and the contribution of each equity return component. We see that earnings growth contributed 7.9% pts and 7.5% pts to total return during 2011-24 and 2003-10, respectively. However, while multiples contracted during 2003-10 shaving off 2.3% pts from total return, multiple expansion contributed 3.8% pts (or nearly one-third) to total return during 2011-24. Simply put, annualized returns during 2011-24 were nearly double that during 2003-10, while earnings growth during 2011-24 was only marginally higher than the earlier period. Dividend yield, which tends to be more stable, was similar during both periods.

Exhibit 3: Annualized total return and contribution of valuation expansion, earnings growth and dividend yield, Percent

Period Ann. Total Return PE EPS DY
2011-24 13.6% 3.8% 7.9% 2.0%
2003-10 7.2% -2.3% 7.5% 2.0%

Source: FTSE Russell and LSEG. Data as of 31 December 2024. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Note: The total return for each year is indicated in the box. PE, EPS and DY show the valuation expansion, earnings growth and dividend yield components of total return, respectively.

This shows that since 2010, valuation expansion has played a greater role in US equity performance than previously. In that context, the contribution of multiple expansion to total return over the last two years is not an anomaly. What is an anomaly is that compared to the recovery years post-recession in a business cycle, the market recovery over the last two years were different, where the rallies were still driven primarily by valuation expansion and not earnings growth. This anomaly could potentially point to correction risks in US equities.

Conclusion

The large US equity rallies in 2023 and 2024 have been driven mainly by valuation expansion. In one sense that is a continuation of the structural trend in recent history, post the European debt crisis, when valuation expansion has played a larger role in driving US equity returns than previously. Yet it is different from cyclical trends, where post-recession (or recession fear years) where the initial rally is driven by valuation expansion, but earnings growth picks up the baton very soon. In the market rally after the 2022 drawdown, valuation expansion has yet to turn the baton over to earnings growth as the main driver.

Given this predominance of multiple expansion as a driver of equity returns over the past two years, it suggests that US equity market fortunes may be more susceptible to changes in investor sentiment, particularly as it relates to the AI-fuelled tech rally. We saw an example of this in January 2025, when the release of the Chinese DeepSeek AI model, that was reportedly trained at a fraction of the cost of similar purpose US AI models, brought the tech rally to a halt. Tech stocks in the large-cap index returned zero in aggregate over January 2025. Technology was the worst performing equity industry (based on ICB industries) and US equities lagged developed market peers in Europe, the UK and Asia Pacific in January 2025. It also implies that investors could potentially face greater equity market volatility going forward.

1. We start our analysis in 2003 due to the availability of component data for index total return. The Russell 1000 index recorded negative total return in calendar years 2000 (-7.8%), 2001 (-12.4%) and 2002 (-21.7%), making 2003 the first calendar year of positive market returns after the bursting of the Tech Bubble.

2. As measured by the cumulative total returns of the FTSE USA index (507%) and the FTSE All-World ex USA index (95%) between end-2010 and end-2024.
